data.title: Cenovus: Bargain With An Excellent Shareholder Yield
data.description:
Article Thesis

Cenovus Energy Inc. (NYSE:CVE) reported its Q4 results on Thursday, with the report showing relatively weak profitability due to bad results from CVE's refining business. Still, cash generation in 2024 remained strong, and I believe that cash generation will be healthy in the current year as well. With an undemanding valuation, some production growth, and compelling expected shareholder returns, Cenovus Energy could be a good investment for 2025 and beyond.

Past Coverage

I have written about Cenovus Energy Inc. here on Seeking Alpha in the past. My most recent article on the company is from early December 2024, close to three months ago. I was bullish on the company back then and remain so despite the rather weak profitability shown for the fourth quarter. Shares got even cheaper since my most recent article on CVE, due, in part, to worries about tariffs against Canada -- I believe these worries are overblown. With these macro fears making Cenovus even cheaper than before, and with the company having reported its most recent earnings results this week, it is time for an update on this Canadian oil player.

Cenovus: Macro Fears Make For A Buying Opportunity

Shares of Cenovus Energy are down over the last month and on a year-to-date basis, despite the fact that the broad market has rallied to new all-time highs. This relatively weak share price performance in 2025 has been driven by investors' worries about what the US administration could do to Cenovus Energy and its Canadian energy peers. While the new US administration is for less regulation and more energy production, which generally is good for energy companies, President Donald Trump has argued that he could increase tariffs on goods that Canada (and Mexico) export to the US. While tariffs have been postponed for now, thanks to the Canadian government agreeing on more border security measures, some investors still fear that the Canadian energy industry could be hurt by the new US administration. And it is true -- that might happen, as it is not impossible. But I still believe that worries are overblown:

- First, it is in the best interest of President Donald Trump to have as much oil flowing into the US as possible, as it is his goal to bring down energy prices. Tariffs on oil imports would interfere with this goal.

- Second, talking about tariffs could be a negotiating tool -- and with the Canadian and Mexican government beefing up their border security, it looks like this has worked for now.

I believe that it is far from guaranteed that tariffs will be instated at all, and even if that happens, I believe that it is likely that oil will have a lower tariff rate compared to other goods.

If tariffs were instated, and if they were to include oil, then Cenovus Energy could still decide to sell its oil to other markets -- energy-hungry and fast-growing countries in Asia, for example, could buy more oil from CVE and its Canadian peers.

While there is some lingering risk of tariffs having a negative impact on CVE at some point in the future, I do not believe that this is a likely scenario. And even if that were to happen, CVE could export more oil elsewhere, thus tariffs would not be a disastrous outcome.

Cenovus' Q4: Weaker Profitability

Cenovus Energy reported its fourth-quarter earnings results on Thursday. Profitability, unfortunately, wasn't very strong, and the same can be said for CVE's free cash flow. The company saw its revenues decline slightly, driven by lower refining margins and lower oil prices, but profits took a much bigger hit -- the company earned just CAD$150 million during the period on a net basis. But while that sounds far from good, there are some mitigating factors to consider. First, some of the profit hits were either one-time items (such as refinery turnaround spending that doesn't occur every quarter), while there were also pure accounting items due to first-in, first-out accounting. Last but not least, the fourth quarter is usually a weaker quarter for Cenovus Energy, due to seasonal patterns in the Chicago refining market -- where Q4 tends to be a quarter of low profitability. The rather low profitability during the fourth quarter is thus not an indictment of CVE, but rather the result of some headwinds combining -- seasonal patterns, accounting items, some market weakness, and so on.

Despite these issues, Cenovus Energy's adjusted funds from operations totaled CAD$1.6 billion during the quarter, or easily more than CAD$6 billion annualized. Throughout all of 2024, including the seasonally stronger quarters, CVE's funds from operations totaled CAD$8.2 billion, down only slightly versus fiscal 2023 (when oil prices were higher).

CVE: Cash Flows Could Be Higher This Year

With Cenovus benefitting from stronger seasonal patterns in the coming quarters, and with CVE forecasting some production growth in 2025 -- which will mean higher revenue, higher profit, and higher cash flow, all else equal -- I would not be surprised to see this number grow to more than CAD$8.2 billion this year. According to YCharts, CVE is forecasted to see its EBITDA grow by around US$500 million this year, which would be equal to around CAD$700 million. CVE has reduced its net debt in recent years, which has made its interest expense decline, which should also be positive for its profits and cash generation this year. Between these factors, a CAD$9.0 billion funds from operations number might be achievable, but let's be more conservative and assume FFO will only hit CAD$8.5 billion -- which would be up just CAD$0.3 billion.

In 2024, CVE has spent CAD$5.0 billion on capital expenditures such as its West White Rose project. In 2025, capital spending will likely be a bit lower: The capital budget is CAD$4.6 billion to CAD5.0 billion, so CAD$4.8 billion at the midpoint. While free funds flow totaled CAD$3.15 billion last year, free funds flow could rise to around CAD$3.65 billion or so this year -- thanks to higher funds from operations and lower capital spending. CVE is trading with a market capitalization of CAD$38 billion, thus this makes for a roughly 10x free funds flow multiple -- where growth spending is already accounted for. This means that CVE's free funds flow yield could be in the 10% range this year, which should allow Cenovus to return a lot of cash to its owners. The dividend yield is in the 3% range right now, suggesting that CVE might buy back around 7% of its shares this year if the valuation remains as low as it is right now.

For 2026, things could be even better: Cenovus' CEO suggested that capital spending will decline further in 2026, while funds from operations will hopefully be higher as the refining market improves and additional growth projects come online. Higher operating cash flow and lower capital spending could lift CVE's free funds flow to well above CAD$3.65 billion next year, potentially allowing for a shareholder yield of substantially more than 10% -- and potential tailwinds from a rising oil price, e.g. due to Chinese growth resuming, are not yet accounted for.

CVE: Attractively Priced

There are some risks when investing in CVE: Politics could be a headwind, although, as shown above, I do not think this is likely. When a company operates assets such as refineries, there is also the risk of accidents -- although CVE has gotten better with that. Cenovus has a strong balance sheet, thus it could stomach some headwinds should they occur -- overall, while CVE isn't risk-free, I do not consider it a high-risk stock.

Cenovus Energy never was expensive, but it got even cheaper in the recent past:

Based on current estimates for this year, the enterprise value to EBITDA multiple is barely above 4 right now. With the longer-term average being significantly above 5, CVE trades at a huge discount to its historically normal valuation. With free cash flow likely improving this year, CVE can take advantage of this low valuation by buying back its shares at a rapid pace, while I also believe that we will get another dividend increase this year.

Between production growth, declining capital spending, improving free cash flow, potential for hefty shareholder returns, and a very low valuation, I believe that CVE could offer compelling total returns going forward -- and the shareholder yield that could be in the double-digits this year and beyond will be a key driver for those total returns.

data.title: 8 Stocks I'm Buying Amid A Market Rotation Toward Dividend Stocks
data.description:
Every year, the market deals investors a different set of cards than it did the year before.

Sometimes only slightly different, sometimes majorly different.

So far this year, Mr. Market seems to be dealing a subtly different hand to investors than he did in 2023-2024. And happily for us dividend investors, the cards appear to be largely in our favor thus far.

Welcome back to my weekly newsletter-y article, in which I cover major points of interest in the economy and markets as well as my regularly updated dividend stock buy list.

Let's get to it.

Here's what's on the docket this week:

  • A rotation seems to be occurring in the market, favoring non-tech dividend stocks and disfavoring the aggrandized Magnificent 7.
  • A dive into Elon Musk's DOGE, and why I consider it mostly "sound and fury signifying nothing."
  • My five favorite metrics by which to monitor the US economy going forward.
  • My 8-stock buy list.

Onward.

Market Rotation Monitor

The turn of calendar years can sometimes be a catalyst for market rotations and shifts in market trajectories.

For example, the turn from 2021 to 2022 saw a sharp reversal in market trends, as high-tech and rate-sensitive stocks immediately began pricing in rising interest rates at the beginning of 2022. Stable and defensive sectors like consumer staples (VDC) and utilities (XLU) massively outperformed both the tech stocks in the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) and real estate stocks in the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ):

Talk about a market rotation!

So far this year, the winds have been shifting somewhat, but not to the same degree as in 2022.

What I as a dividend growth investor ("DGIer") find fascinating is that all of the dividend ETFs I own are outperforming the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) so far this year. Admittedly, that's a short measurement period, but if it persists, it will prove that the stock market really is rotating.

Aside from the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), which I think is temporarily underperforming due to idiosyncratic stock selection (that should be at least partially alleviated with its March reconstitution), the interesting thing to note here is that the dividend ETFs with the least exposure to the Mag 7 are performing the best year-to-date.

The Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) both have zero exposure to Mag 7 stocks. The iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) has a little exposure, and the WisdomTree US Quality Dividend Growth ETF (DGRW) has a little more.

But even within the Mag 7, performance has varied dramatically this year, ranging from Meta Platforms' (META) 20% appreciation to Tesla's (TSLA) >10% drop.

Aside from Meta and Apple (AAPL), the turning point for most of these stocks came when DeepSeek's R1 model was announced on January 20th.

The collective ~$300 billion in capex announced by Mag 7 companies for 2025, combined with the DeepSeek-inspired notion that it might be possible to do more with less in the AI space, has undoubtedly put a damper on investor enthusiasm for these mega-cap stocks.

For DGIers who think this Mag 7 consolidation is a good long-term buying opportunity, DGRW is probably the best ETF to allocate capital today.

For those who think the market rotation will continue, broad-based dividend ETFs with little to no exposure to the Mag 7 like VYM, HDV, and DGRO are probably the better bet.

DOGE: Signal Or Noise?

While government efficiency is absolutely a good idea, and increasing it a worthy endeavor, several points suggest that Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency ("DOGE") is more noise than signal, in my opinion.

That is, it is more an exercise in political theater than a serious effort to reduce government waste -- or government spending more broadly.

The first point to note is that DOGE is populated by employees, including Musk, with no experience in the federal government or government budgetary analysis. As a result, they are making mistakes that government budgetary experts likely would not.

For example, DOGE recently claimed savings of $8 billion for an ICE contract pertaining to its "office of diversity and civil rights." That would make ICE's office of diversity and civil rights as well-funded as the entire CDC. But actually, the contract was only for $8 million, a mistake that would have been obvious to someone with experience in government budgets.

Moreover, since the multi-year contract was partially completed already, actual savings are only $5.5 million from canceling it.

The $55 billion of total savings claimed by DOGE is the total original contract value of cancelled spending. Actual savings are significantly less.

Analysis from the New York Times indicates that there are dozens if not hundreds of similar instances of inflated savings claims.

Add up the itemized savings list and you get only $8.6 billion in actual savings, far lower than the touted $55 billion.

Another point to consider is that the vast majority of cancelled spending thus far has been the most politically palatable items to cut. But there simply isn't enough runway among DEI programs, Politico subscriptions, and foreign aid to reach $1 trillion, much less balance the budget (which would require ~$2 trillion to be cut).

Lastly, it is notable that DOGE is being led by a major political donor to the president and is named after a memecoin that began as a joke in the crypto community.

Regardless of one's personal politics, does that sound like the makings of a serious effort to root out government waste?

A government office does exist to do exactly what DOGE is now trying to do. It's called the Government Accountability Office ("GAO"). Last year, the GAO released a study of fiscal years 2018-2022, finding that a range of 3-7% of total federal spending every year verifiably qualifies as waste, fraud, abuse, or mismanagement. That amounted to $233 billion to $521 billion annually.

The GAO investigates thousands of claims of alleged government waste, fraud, abuse, and mismanagement every year, and instances that are actually verifiable are then referred to the appropriate government agency.

It seems like the GAO would be a useful agency to consult in an effort to root out government waste, fraud, and abuse. So is DOGE working closely with the GAO?

No. According to GAO Director Rebecca Shea, a three-decade veteran of the agency, the GAO has had no communication with DOGE, and no one from DOGE has reached out to the GAO.

All of the above leads me to believe that DOGE is more noise than signal. Trump and Musk are both masters of marketing, in my opinion, and my impression is that DOGE's primary function is to rack up marketable wins by targeting the discretionary corner of the federal budget.

Why should this matter to investors?

Because if federal government spending did see deep cuts, on net, then the resulting economic effect would likely be lower inflation and interest rates, which in turn would result in a boost to stocks (aside from those with hefty government contracts, perhaps).

The vast majority of federal government spending is mandatory items such as Medicare, Medicaid, Social Security, and interest expense, as well as various military programs -- not DEI training or Politico subscriptions.

It remains highly unlikely, in my view, that either Congress or the President will be willing to make significant cuts in these areas.

On top of that, the CRFB recently estimated that the impact of Trump's tax proposals would be an additional $5-11 trillion of debt over the next 10 years.

The cost only of the proposed incremental tax cuts on top of extending the Tax Cuts & Jobs Act is between $1.1 trillion and $6.4 trillion over 10 years -- or between $110 billion and $640 billion per year.

Now add to that about $150 billion per year in additional spending on border security and immigration enforcement.

Senator Rand Paul, known as a fiscal hawk, was the sole Republican to vote against the budget being advanced in Congress right now, because he alleged that it would increase spending by $342 billion (+5% over fiscal 2024).

Rather than a Javier Milei-esque shift toward austerity and efficiency, investors would do well to expect more of the same from Washington DC.

Five Metrics To Monitor The Economy

When it comes to gauging the overall health of the economy, I believe there are five primary metrics to watch.

1. Housing Starts

As I discussed in last week's article, "7 Stocks & 1 ETF I'm Buying As Inflation Is Lower Than It Looks," housing starts are an incredibly important forward indicator of the direction of economic growth.

When housing starts are increasing, economic growth stays strong for a while as homebuilders and homebuyers continue to spend.

Last week, I mentioned that if December's jump to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.5 million housing starts persists, then a recession will almost certainly be avoided and "soft landing" achieved. But unfortunately, January's recently reported residential construction report showed no such thing. Annualized starts slumped back to 1.37 million.

That's still a decent level of starts, mostly concentrated in single-family homes, and it signals neither acceleration nor deceleration in economic growth.

2. Dallas Fed Weekly Economic Index

The Weekly Economic Index is a nice way to keep tabs on the economy in a fairly up-to-date way, as it is a composite measure of 10 different daily or weekly reported indicators. These include same-store retail sales, unemployment claims, payroll tax receipts, electricity output, fuel sales, and railroad volume.

The WEI peaked in the first half of 2021, as economic reopening and massive fiscal stimulus fueled a red-hot post-COVID rebound. It steadily cooled over the course of 2021 and 2022 and has more or less plateaued since then.

The current ~2.5 reading is above the post-GFC / pre-COVID average level of ~2, indicating a moderately healthy economy.

3. Conference Board Coincident Index

The Conference Board's coincident index is based on four indicators: payroll employment, personal income excluding transfer payments, manufacturing & trade sales, and industrial production.

Here again, we find that the coincident index has basically plateaued over the last few years, indicating a steady and moderately healthy economy.

I could stop at these three indicators, because the next two are contained within them. But I think the next two are important enough to be worthy of specifically monitoring.

4. Total Employment (Nonfarm Payrolls)

The economy basically grows when the labor force grows and/or when labor productivity grows. The economy can still grow if the labor force isn't growing, but lack of labor force growth is a significant headwind.

That's what makes it interesting to find that nonfarm payrolls growth has steadily slowed over the last several years.

Over the last six months, payrolls growth appears to have bottomed and slightly rebounded. We'll see if that employment rebound persists.

5. Industrial Production

Industrial production is a very wide-ranging index including manufacturing, mining, and electricity generation.

I believe this is a very important metric to watch during Trump's second term, as his American First policies should result in a firming of growth in industrial production, if successful. But, of course, they could also backfire, as his tariff policies seemed to do in late 2018 and 2019.

Industrial production enjoyed a nice bounce in its most recent reading, which came after Trump's electoral victory. Whether it persists as new tariffs take effect will be interesting to find out.

Honorable Mention: Manufacturing Capacity Utilization

Drilling down to an even more specific indication of whether Trump's America First economic policies are working as intended, I'd add manufacturing capacity utilization as an honorable mention.

This measures the level of factory production capacity is actually be used for production. The more use factory machinery is getting to pump out goods, the stronger the manufacturing sector of the economy is.

Manufacturing capacity utilization peaked in early 2022 and has steadily declined since then.

The long-term average manufacturing capacity utilization from 1972 through 2024 is 78.2%, so the current 76.2% is about 2.5% below the long-term average.

Notice that after Trump's first round of tariffs were imposed in September 2018, US manufacturing capacity utilization declined. That is because there are more US manufacturers that benefit from lower-cost imported inputs than there are manufacturers that are directly protected and benefited by the tariffs.

It will be interesting to see whether the same pattern repeats this time if and when more tariffs are imposed.

My Buy List Today

I am a long-term buy-and-hold dividend growth investor with a Buffett-esque ownership philosophy. I seek to own high-quality businesses with durable competitive advantages and business models that I understand well.

Since my ideal holding period is "lifelong," I think a lot more about dividends and dividend growth than total returns. My portfolio turnover is very low, which is exactly what it should be if I have chosen my investments well. And given my work background in real estate, I maintain an outsized ~40% portfolio weighting in REITs, which are my wheelhouse.

With that preamble out of the way, here's my current buy list:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.4% High-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.4% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Artesian Resources (ARTNA) 3.8% Mid-Single-Digit
CareTrust REIT (CTRE) 4.5% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Healthpeak Properties (DOC) 6.3% Low-Single-Digit
Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) 5.8% Mid-Single-Digit
NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE) 3.3% High-Single-Digit
Essential Utilities (WTRG) 3.6% Mid-Single-Digit

I discussed recent news on AMH, CTRE, DOC, and HASI in last week's article.

NEE came back onto my buy list because, after its expected 10% dividend hike for this year, I recalculated its projected 10-year yield-on-cost based on one more year of 10% dividend growth, followed by 8% growth thereafter. It surpassed my target 10-year YoC of at least 7%, so I put it back on my buy list.

NEE's dividend growth rate beyond 2026 would need to drop below 7.75% annually to fall below my buy threshold.

Given the company's A- credit rating, modest debt load (for a utility), ~60% payout ratio, and strong positioning as a developer of (badly needed) power generation facilities, I believe NEE should be able to continue growing its dividend by at least a high-single-digit rate for the foreseeable future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How To Invest $100,000 In A Near-Perfect Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction:

The market has been in a bull market for the past about 30 months now. The broad market index, the S&P 500 (SP500), has advanced from a low of SPX3600 in Oct.2022 to a high of SPX6114 on Feb. 14, 2025, an increase of nearly 70% from the bottom. The overall market appears to have become quite overvalued based on several metrics, and it is currently taking a pause as there is some level of uncertainty about the future direction. President Trump's administration offers many positives and tailwinds for the market, such as less regulation, lower energy prices, and 2017 tax cuts likely becoming permanent. However, it has also added plenty of uncertainties, especially regarding tariffs and global trade adjustments. To top it all, the Fed has indicated that inflation may remain sticky and seems to be in no hurry to lower the interest rates. All that makes the market environment quite uncertain and challenging.

All that said, we believe it is never a good idea to keep a lot of cash or stay uninvested. The simple reason is that cash earns little, and whatever it earns rarely exceeds the rate of inflation. So, we always like to stay invested. But what if you have fresh capital or are moving funds from a 401K account to a self-managed IRA? The big question that often comes up is how and where to invest. For example, if we were to have $100,000 cash to invest today, where should we invest? That's what this article will attempt to address.

What is a NPS (Near-Perfect Strategy) Portfolio?

It is a bucket-based strategy with three unique sub-strategies. It is simply a combination of DGI (Dividend Growth Investing), High-Income, and a Hedging strategy. Put together, these three buckets provide income, growth, and conservation of capital. The goal is to significantly lower volatility and drawdowns compared to the broader market. Lower drawdowns and consistent income help to make the ride easier during deep corrections or recessions. The structure provides flexibility to the investor on how much they want to allocate to each of the buckets based on their personal needs and goals.

With the above background, we can define the following goals for our NPS portfolio:-

  • The primary objective of this portfolio will be to provide a 5% to 6% income that is consistent and reliable.
  • The portfolio should grow (total returns) at least 6% to 7% over and above the rate of inflation), over the long term.
  • The overall portfolio should provide lower volatility than the broader market and drawdowns that are no more than 70% to 75% of the broader market.

Who Should Invest in a NPS Portfolio

The following group of folks should consider this type of portfolio:

  • Anyone who likes to achieve financial freedom.
  • Retirees or anyone who plans to retire in the next 10 years.
  • Anyone above the age of 50 who wants to transition from a growth-oriented or index-based portfolio to an income and growth portfolio but with less volatility and lower drawdowns.

The NPS Portfolio:

Here is the structure of the NPS portfolio. It will have three buckets. For this article, we will assume roughly equal allocation to each of the three buckets, but this is flexible based on personal preference and goals.

  • DGI bucket (to provide roughly 3% Income and good growth)
  • High-Income Funds Bucket with at least 8% Income
  • Hedging Bucket (to conserve capital and still provide decent growth).

(I) The DGI Bucket: (35% of the assets)

To start a new DGI portfolio bucket, you generally have two options. You could invest in individual dividend stocks or dividend-paying funds (ETFs — Exchange-Traded Funds). The choice will be determined by your individual situation and preferences. Folks who want a more hands-off approach should preferably invest in a portfolio of funds. You can read our recent article on how to create a fund-based portfolio.

If you prefer to invest in individual stocks and do not mind devoting some regular time researching and monitoring your positions, then this route is appropriate. Also, there are some distinct advantages to owning individual stocks. The first one is that there are no ongoing fees. Even to buy or sell, most online brokers offer commission-free trades. On the contrary, most funds charge some ongoing fees that can add overtime and have to come from your total returns. The second advantage of owning individual funds is avoiding over-diversification. Sure, diversification is important to avoid risk, but it can quickly lead to over-diversification. We know that over-diversification usually leads to market average returns (minus the fund fees). With funds, especially a portfolio of funds, it is difficult (if not impossible) to avoid over-diversification and some duplication.

When you own individual stocks, you can decide how much you want to diversify. But, we recommend having at least 15 individual stocks to avoid a stock-specific risk.

Below, we will describe a simple method to structure and create a DGI portfolio.

Let's consider the following popular dividend ETFs and look at the top-10 holdings of each fund.

  1. Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD),
  2. Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM),
  3. Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG),
  4. Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV).

SCHD is one of the most popular dividend ETFs. VYM and VIG are two Vanguard dividend ETFs, the first one for higher dividends, while VIG has more growth names. Lastly, FDVV is the dividend fund from Fidelity.

Steps:

  1. Take the top 10 holdings of each of the above funds. In all, we will get 40 stocks.
  2. Sort and weed out the duplicate holdings. We are left with 28 stocks with an average dividend yield of roughly 2.60%. This is a good first list.
  3. Remove duplicates based on sector or industry segments, especially if there are too many names from one sector or segment. For example, we had five companies from the healthcare industry. Some level of subjective judgment is necessary in this step.
  4. Finally, we are left with 15 names. The average yield is 2.88%, and the past 10-year dividend growth is 11.77%.
  5. These 15 stocks are presented below.

(BAC), (KO), (HD), (CSCO), (MSFT), (BLK), (PFE), (ABBV), (UNH), (CVX), (AVGO), (V), (PG), (PM), and (VZ).

Table-1: DGI Bucket

(II) The High-Income Bucket: (35% of the assets)

Now, we know that the DGI bucket is going to provide us income somewhere in the range of 3% to 4%, but that may not be enough income for many folks. This bucket will invest some high-income funds that will aim for 7% to 8% income and elevate the overall income yield of the portfolio. These funds would be drawn mainly from three categories: CEFs (Closed-End funds), REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts), and BDCs (Business Development Companies). In addition to the high-income yield, these funds would add a layer of diversification that we could not have achieved from the DGI bucket. This will add a number of new asset classes that may be missing if we only had the DGI bucket.

We selected the following 10 funds:- a combination of ETFs (Exchange-Traded funds), traditional mutual funds, and CEFs. One of the main criteria of selection is the past track record of the funds and the income yield that they provide:

(FAGIX), (ADX), (PEO), (AMLP), (JEPQ), (MPV), (RFI), (JFNIX), (BIZD), and (UTG).

Table-2: High-Income Bucket

Note: In the above table, there is one CEF that is trading at a significant premium (MPV at 17%). We would not recommend buying this at such a high premium, and it may be best to accumulate this in many installments rather than a lump sum (or wait until the premium gets smaller).

(III) The Hedging Bucket: (30% of the assets)

As such, this bucket is optional. The first two buckets (DGI and High-income) can provide an investor with more than enough diversification, growth, and income. However, there is one aspect that is missing from the first two buckets, and that is “hedging and downside protection.” For some investors, that is not important as they can tolerate the ups and downs of the market. But for many, especially retirees, it can be a big problem. Many times, some retirees just put aside enough cash for their 2–3 years of expenses, and that works well for them. But we also know that cash barely earns more than the rate of inflation. So, keeping a large amount of cash is probably not the best option. To address this issue, we will add a third bucket that is specifically designed to provide hedging and downside protection without incurring any ongoing costs.

For this bucket, we will have a pool of six securities, each representing a specific asset class. Based on a momentum formula, we will buy two securities every month (and hold them for the next month). The catch here is that these asset classes have low correlation among them and can move in different directions. For example, if the stocks are trending down, GOLD may be trending up or vice versa. All it means is that they are not co-related. This model tries to capture that and tries to stay in assets that are trending up and avoid the ones that are trending down. Also, if all other assets are trending down, the model tries to stay in cash.

For this bucket, we will select securities representing Equities, Gold, Real estate, Treasuries, Commodities, and Cash (parked in a money-market fund).

  1. ProShares Ultra QQQ ETF (QLD)
  2. SPDR Glod Shares ETF (GLD)
  3. iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT)
  4. Cohen & Steers Real-estate & Preferred Fund (RNP)
  5. Vanguard Materials ETF (VAW)
  6. Cash (or Money market fund).

Table-3: List of six securities:

Note: QLD is an ultra QQQ (Nasdaq-100), and it aims to provide 2x returns of QQQ after fees. We do not recommend QLD as a buy-and-hold investment, but this is inside the rotation portfolio, which means it will be held only during times when there is strong positive momentum. More conservative investors could replace QLD with QQQ (with expectations of lower returns).

2. Not all securities in this model provide significant income. Moreover, since we are not constantly invested

in any one security, we may miss the dividend payments. So, income investors who need to draw income should aim to withdraw up to 5% (annualized) income at the end of each year.

The monthly rotation process:

At the end of each month, we will calculate the performance (total returns, including any dividends or distributions) of all six securities over the last 3 months. We will then select the top two securities based on total performance. The capital in this hedging bucket is then invested in these two securities, divided equally (50% each) for the next month. This process is repeated every month.

Backtested Performance – Hedging/Rotational Bucket:

With the six securities that we have chosen, we can go back to January 2007, which covers a period of 18-plus years. For backtesting, obviously we want to cover many types of environments. The last 18 years do cover a few significant events, such as the 2008-2009 financial crisis, the 2014-2015 energy prices crash, the 2020 pandemic, the 2022 tech crash, as well as other factors like zero interest rates and high interest rates.

Chart-1: Growth of $100,000, Rotational Model vs. S&P 500

Table-4: Performance metrics (Model vs. S&P500)

Risks and potential concerns:

There are several risks and downsides that an investor should be aware of before investing in the strategy described above. First, there is always the market risk. After all, nearly 90% of investments are in stocks. Despite the hedging bucket (which covers only a third of the portfolio), our investments will be subject to downside risk if the overall market takes a dive. Second, due to the presence of a 'Rotation' bucket, it requires monthly rotations and some level of ongoing work. Any sort of inconsistency can reduce the potential of hedging. A rotation bucket would also add to tax complications unless it is performed inside a tax-deferred account (like an IRA account).

Concluding Thoughts:

At first glance, a portfolio with three unique buckets/strategies may appear to be a lot of work. But when you look at it, the first two buckets are simply 'buy-and-hold' with little work once they have been established. The third bucket (rotational) requires some work, but no more than an hour of work on a monthly basis. Furthermore, it provides the investor all the flexibility as to how much they want to allocate to each bucket based on their personal situation and goals. In addition, since the total returns from the buy-and-hold buckets will be subject to valuations at the time of buying, it is important to establish them over a period of time to avoid buying at or near the top.

The portfolio can comfortably provide a 5% to 6% income to retirees and income investors, market-matching total returns with lower volatility and drawdowns.

data.title: Nu Holdings: Deposit Surge, Net Interest Margins Squeeze. A Risky Balance
data.description:
This is my first coverage of Nu Holdings Ltd. (NYSE:NU). The stock emerged on my radar following the Q4 2024 panic selloff, which led to a 19% discount on its share price.

From a valuation perspective, even after the selloff, the company seems quite expensive if we consider P/B as the comparison metric. In fact, Nu is the most expensive company (by this metric) within the diversified banking industry.

In this article, I will discuss several factors that deterred me from buying the dip, among them a contraction in NIM despite a significant increase in deposits in Mexico and Colombia.

The stock price is at a critical point. If it drops below the long-term support level, I expect it to fall to the low $8 range. However, it's too soon to call this a sell. For now, it's best to hold.

The Downside

The company released Q4 and FY 2024 earnings results on February 20, after market close. I see several concerning drivers that led to the 19% selloff.

First, the company reported a contraction in NIM (net interest margins) for the third consecutive time since Q2 2024.

Out of the 70bps decline in Q4 2024, about 45% was due to unfavorable FX rates. A quick look at the chart below shows the dominance of the US Dollar over the Brazilian Real in FY 2024. However, note that since January 2025, there has been a significant pullback.

The next two drivers accounted for the remaining 55% of the NIM contraction, in roughly the same relative weight:

The remaining 55% of the contraction is largely equally explained by two things. Number one, the drop in yields in the overall credit portfolio in Brazil as we grow secured lending more than we grow unsecured lending in terms of loan balance. And two, as a result of the growing deposits in Mexico and Colombia, which are still paying kind of interest that are higher than the intrabank deposit rates. - Guilherme Lago, Q4 2024 earnings call.

The shift in the loan portfolio mix toward secured lending (i.e. payroll & FGTS-backed loans) carries a lower interest yield when compared to unsecured lending (i.e. personal and SME loans). To be specific, total secured lending grew by 615% yoy in 2024, totaling $1.4 billion in portfolio size. This is approximately 23% of the company's total lending portfolio.

In regard to the growing deposits in Mexico and Colombia, the company used high deposit rates as a customer acquisition strategy. Naturally, this leads to an increase in the cost of funds, which lowers interest spreads. To be specific, deposits in Mexico increased by 438% yoy, totaling $4.5 billion. In Colombia, deposits reached $1.3 billion in just two quarters.

The next disappointing result comes from a decline in the loan to deposit ratio of 1 percentage point qoq, totaling 39%. Total deposits increased by 55% yoy (on a FX neutral basis), while the lending portfolio only grew by 45% yoy. Why is this trend disappointing? Well, these deposits generate costs (interest paid on customer balances). If the bank is not using them to lend money, this can lead to net interest income being pressured. Until these deposits are (of course, not the entire amount) deployed into loans, NIM could remain compressed.

Another point to highlight during the earnings call was the strategic slowdown in PIX financing. Management confirmed PIX financing remains highly profitable and in strong demand, however, Nu is deliberately slowing down eligibility expansion for this product.

... we basically reduced the eligibility growth of PIX financing for the lowest kind of credit bands of financing, primarily to address what we saw some deteriorations in second-order impacts related primarily to NPS, churn, and engagement.

The company is now more selective with lower credit bands for PIX financing, which makes sense considering that credit loss allowances expenses increased by 12% qoq, totaling $804M in Q4 2024.

Related to this topic, I considered including below an excerpt from the Q&A.

Citi analyst Gustavo Schroden asked the following:

We've seen some signals of a potential deterioration in inflation, inflation for foods and we know that there is a high correlation between inflation and asset quality, also an environment of high interest rates, some questions about the macroeconomic environment, and we've seen mean the system or most of banks that we talk to adopting a more conservative approach on the credit concession... ... I'd like to understand what's the difference here, and if you do not have concerns about potential adverse selection in this environment.

COO Youssef Lahrech replied the following:

And so this is why we always say that when we underwrite a loan or a credit card or any form of credit, we expect the future to be significantly worse than the past, right? Those are the assumptions that we put in our models to underwrite.

Based on the comment above, I believe the increase in credit loss allowances is more proactive rather than the company being reactive to an increase in delinquencies. Nonetheless, this is something to keep an eye on in the next earnings call.

Finally, another factor to keep an eye on is the expansion towards higher-income customers (Ultravioleta credit product), who have lower APRs. Naturally, this negatively affects credit yields. To be specific, Ultravioleta credit purchase volume grew 106% yoy, accounting for 10% of total Brazilian credit card volume.

Is the slowdown in PIX financing of lower credit scores and the increase in Ultravioleta credit purchase volume an early indication of macro headwinds in the Brazilian economy? In my view, it could be, however, I believe it's too early to have certainty.

From a valuation perspective, the company has a FWD P/B ratio of 5.9. That's more than 3x of the financials sector median. In fact, if we look at the top diversified bank industry, Nu is the most expensive when looking at P/B.

The Upside

Despite possible macroeconomic uncertainties in the Brazilian economy, the company's NPL coverage is over 200%, which significantly mitigates default risks.

As I said before, the loan portfolio growth was up 45% yoy. Even though this was a lower increase than overall deposits (on a FX neutral basis), this high double-digit growth is something that I still see as positive. Additionally, I believe the increase in deposits, especially in Mexico and Colombia, could eventually lead to conversions into the loan portfolio later this year.

Another positive factor is the steady increase in ROE, despite the increase in secured lending.

Additionally, net margins seem to be quite healthy, despite a qoq decline in Q4 2024.

From a price action perspective, following the Q4 2024 selloff, the share price is trading close to a support trend line. I'll keep a close eye on this stock over the next few weeks, as a breakout below this support line could indicate a downturn in investor sentiment, which can lead to a further decline, down to $8 level (which seems to be the next historical support level).

Conclusion

To wrap up, I rate Nu Holdings as a hold. I'll be keeping a close eye on the share price over the next few weeks, and any news coming from Brazil regarding macroeconomic factors that could lead to an increase in delinquency rates. For the moment, the company's NPL coverage is over 200%, which is a positive sign. If the credit yield keeps declining over the next quarters, I believe there is a good chance to see the share price trading in the low $8s.

data.title: The RealReal: Strong Customer Adds, But The Stock Has Run Too Hot (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
With the S&P 500 continuing to dance around all-time highs, I continue to advocate for two main portfolio moves: rotating out of momentum-driven winners that delivered big gains in 2024, and allocating more of our portfolios toward "growth at a reasonable price" stocks. Often, this means jumping into more speculative rebound plays: but the trick is when to lock in gains when these moves turn successful.

The RealReal (NASDAQ:REAL), an online consignment marketplace, has been a very positive trade over the past year. The struggling e-commerce name has now seen its share prices nearly quadruple over the past year as it successfully exited its direct-sales business and returned its focus on pure consignment. And yet, the stock fell ~15% after reporting Q4 results - an indication that the stock is running too hot against heightened expectations.

I last wrote a bullish note on The RealReal in November, when the stock was trading in the mid-$3s. Since then, my stake in The RealReal has nearly doubled, and I'm ready to pull the plug and retreat to the sidelines as the stock comes down, and wait for a better price to re-enter. The company's Q4 results showed continuing encouraging signs on active buyer uptake and GMV expansion, but most of this strength is already priced into The RealReal's stock. As such, I'm downgrading my stance on The RealReal to neutral.

At current share prices, I now see a more balanced bull and bear case for The RealReal. On the positive side for the company:

  • Smart decisions to focus on consignments has improved The RealReal's margin profile. The company's conscious decision to eliminate direct-sales and unwind its inventory is proving successful. GMV is growing y/y again, and consignment sales are growing at an even quicker pace. In addition, the revenue mix shift toward consignment and away from direct sales has pushed The RealReal's gross margin profile above 70%.
  • Trusted name in luxury. The main specter of competition for every company in the real estate space is Amazon (AMZN) - and yet, The RealReal's focus on the luxury category positions the company in a category that Amazon has struggled to gain legitimacy in.

At the same time, however, I view a number of risks for The RealReal that balance out its opportunities:

  • Tough macro conditions for luxury. Pinched by inflation and an uncertain economy, many luxury brands have reported weaker sales. This slowdown is being felt particularly in China, which historically has been the most enthusiastic buyer of luxury goods.
  • Competition. The RealReal has a wide swath of competition in its space, ranging from purchasing directly from fashion houses to other auction/consignment names like Postmark.

All in all, I see an opportunity to take gains on this trade and retreat to the sidelines until The RealReal's share price recedes further.

Q4 download

Let's now go through The RealReal's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

The RealReal's revenue grew 14% y/y to $164.0 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $164.2 million (+15% y/y) by a hair. We do note, however, that The RealReal has continued to accelerate its revenue growth profile, sequentially improving 3 points from an 11% y/y growth rate in Q3.

Underpinning the company's improvement in revenue is continued recovery in GMV trends, which increased 12% y/y to $503 million. We note that GMV accelerated substantially versus 6% growth in Q3, which is a very healthy signal during the holiday quarter, especially when many luxury brands reported weaker-than-expected holiday results (suggesting that The RealReal and its customer base may have the potential to buck macro trends).

Also importantly as well, the company managed to improve its count of active buyers, adding 19k net-new buyers in the quarter to end with 408k active buyers who made a purchase in the trailing three months.

The company's primary strategy for growth consists of leaning more on its sales reps, which forge direct relationships with large-volume consignors. It views a healthy supply on its marketplace as the key constraint on revenue growth, and in FY24 the company experimented with new incentive structures to encourage better performance. The company notes that it's entering into FY25 with better sales rep retention, which could be a factor for better supply growth and overall expansion this year. Per CEO Rati Levesque's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Let's start with the growth playbook. The first component of our growth playbook is our sales team, which is a strategic differentiator. As a reminder, our sales team is made up of our luxury managers who work directly with our consignors to build trust and maximize supply.

Over the course of 2024, we spent time optimizing our incentive structure and elevating our sales team's experience. We better aligned the team's compensation with our overall company goal of driving profitable supply, focusing the team on value rather than units.

Better compensation alignment and elevating the employee experience have resulted in more supply value per luxury manager, lower attrition and higher sales team retention compared to last year. Today, more than half of our sales team has now been with The RealReal for over two years.

We expect these changes to drive results through deeper relationships with sellers, higher approval rating and a better seller experience. Notably, the value generated per sales rep in 2024 was up roughly 15% compared to the prior year."

The company notes that end customer sell-through also remains strong, with the majority of items selling within 90 days on the platform.

On the profitability front, the company improved gross margins by 40bps y/y to 74.4%, a reflection of higher marketplace take rates on growing GMV. Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA expanded 570bps y/y to $11.0 million:

Valuation and key takeaways

To a large extent, however, much of The RealReal's strength is already priced into its stock, even after the post-earnings dip. At current share prices just under $7, the company trades at a market cap of $717.4 million. After we net off the $172.2 million of cash and $303.5 million of debt on The RealReal's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $848.7 million.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, The RealReal has guided to $645-$660 million in revenue, representing 7-10% y/y revenue growth, alongside similar GMV growth rates. It's also pointing to $25 million in midpoint adjusted EBITDA, which represents a near-tripling of adjusted EBITDA on a nominal basis, and a 3.8% margin (or 220bps of expansion this year).

The RealReal trades at 34x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. Of course, the company is still posting nascent profitability and improvement in sales/GMV trends (potentially driven by the company's better salesforce retention) could help the company achieve faster adjusted EBITDA expansion.

For now, however, The RealReal's ~30x multiple against its official outlook already suggests plenty of valuation cushion for outperformance. I'd wait for The RealReal to retreat to levels around $4.50-$5.00 (representing ~25x adjusted EBITDA) before considering buying back in.

data.title: My No. 1 Priority Stock: Why Nvidia's 52% Growth Is Just The Start
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is my #1 priority stock in my personal portfolio for 2025.

Actually, if you include ETFs, my allocation to Nvidia is even higher.

I love Nvidia for so many reasons, including the three that I'll explain today.

The fundamentals of my equity portfolio.

According to Morningstar's analyst consensus, 44% of my portfolio consists of equities with 29% 5-year earnings growth and a very nice 4% yield.

  • SCHD has just 3.5% with 6% growth.

My portfolio's valuation is a very reasonable 18.3X, while the returns on assets and equity (quality metrics) are 50% to 100% better than the S&P average.

In other words, world-class blue chips (Ultra SWANs, in fact) are growing like weeds, paying rich and low-risk dividends, and trading at a PEGY ratio of 0.55.

  • S&P 1.42 (and it's 21% overvalued unless earnings growth is at 11.8% as is currently priced in).

In other words, my portfolio's valuation is 61% cheaper than the S&P 500's when adjusted for yield and growth, thanks to my selection of the best companies for yield, quality, and growth.

  • It's an even better bargain when you consider the 46% better returns on equity and 97% better returns on assets.

How Confident Am I in My Nvidia Position? 1500 Shares Ahead Of Earnings This Quarter!

In 2025 alone, I bought Nvidia seven times before the February 26th earnings report (Wednesday after the bell).

In other words, next week is this earnings season's most important earnings report, as Nvidia is the unofficial captain of the AI tech boom.

It's not just the second-most valuable company in the world, but it will likely set the tone for the next three months if it beats and raises (as it has every quarter since ChatGPT came out in November 2022) or misses and somehow disappoints.

Let me show you the three reasons why I'm confident that Nvidia will beat and raise (like usual). If it disappoints, I'm ready to back up the truck with limit orders to buy more.

I Have Almost $400K Worth Of Limits Set On Nvidia

I expect Nvidia to increase after earnings because it has historically beat EPS by 11% (last four quarters) and remains supply-constrained.

But I reset my limits every quarter (before earnings) to keep Nvidia at my 12.5% max risk cap target allocation.

In other words, if NVDA falls 10%, it falls to 12% of my portfolio, and my limits are programmed to bring it back to 12.5%.

The more Nvidia falls, the more I buy, always bringing it back to 12.5%.

So when Nvidia bottoms and recovers, I thoroughly benefit from its incredible growth.

Last year, this "dynamic rebalancing" increased the gains in my portfolio by 38% (NVDA limit buying contributed 25% to that boost).

Ok, I'm very bullish on Nvidia, including buying 1,500 shares this quarter (a 33% increase in my biggest position).

Now let me show you why.

Reason 1: Has Exceptional Growth Potential For The Next Two Years

Nvidia is a company growing at over 30%, yet it trades at a 22% discount (actually 26%, as I'll explain in reason three).

  • Based on the PEGY ratio.

The growth consensus going into earnings is remarkable.

  • 52% EPS growth in 2025.
  • 25% growth in 2026.

But let me show you why that might be wildly underestimating what NVDA might deliver this year.

In the last year NVDA beat estimates by an average of 11%.

That would be 63% EPS growth if that is sustained.

And for this particular quarter? 64% growth consensus and 75% historical beat expectation.

That's remarkable.

But let me show you why NVDA might beat expectations by its historical 11% this year and possibly by 35% next year.

Nvidia has been supply constrained for years, and things have improved recently.

During 2023, NVDA was constrained by TSMC wager capacity, but Nvidia's CEO noted wafer supply itself is not the bottleneck anymore.

So what is? CoWoS packaging is the bottleneck right now.

CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) combines multiple chips into a single powerful unit.

This is the example of Grace Blackwell, where multiple GPUs are packaged with various interlink technologies and sold as multi-million dollar systems.

TSMC recently announced its CoWoS capacity upgrade plans.

TSMC has announced it will expand CoWoS advanced packaging capacity by over 60% CAGR through 2026.

If NVDA remains supply constrained this year and next year, then its theoretical sales and EPS growth is 60% this year and 60% next year.

That's 156% potential EPS growth courtesy of 160% CoWoS capacity increase from TSMC.

What is the FactSet consensus? 90% EPS growth.

Let me repeat that. In 2025 and 2026 NVDA is expected to almost double EPS (and free cash flow).

But according to management, the #1 limiting factor is CoWoS and TSMC is planning on increasing CoWoS capacity by 156%.

That's 66% higher than current expectation.

Does that mean that NVDA will beat expectations by 66% over the next eight quarters?

  • 6.54% beat per quarter.

No, it means that it could, and do you think it's priced for that? Do you think there is any reasonable way that NVDA grows earnings by 156% in the next two years and investors end up disappointed?

How much multiple compression is possible? For two reasons I think very little is likely, and now I'll explain why.

Reason 2: Nvidia Is Becoming A GPU Utility With Subscription-Based Sales

Tom Lee of Fundastrat has the most optimistic bull-case long-term forecast on Nvidia, "10X in 10 years".

That might sound outlandish, but it actually represents 25% CAGR returns for 10 years.

That's compared to management's guidance of "20% to 25% in AI chip demand growth for the foreseeable future," extending the foreseeable future to 10 years (2035).

But wait a second. Isn't that about $35 trillion in market cap? Yes, it is, but let me explain why Nvidia's historical 40X PE could persist for the next decade if it can maintain the 20% to 25% growth management is discussing "for the foreseeable future."

Nvidia is transitioning from a cyclical hardware company (like Apple was) to a tech utility.

Apple used to trade at low double-digit P/Es until it became a consumer staple with steadily more stable, recurring revenue.

NVDA is not only offering software subscriptions ($4,500 per GPU) to maximize the productivity (and profitability) of its hardware (with 110% CAGR management growth guidance through 2030).

  • $30 billion in AI software subscriptions by 2030 vs $1 billion run rate by end of 2023 and $2 billion by end of 2024.

This isn't going to represent the majority of sales (like at Broadcom) anytime soon, even in 2030 it's likely to be less than 10% (per management guidance).

  • Some analysts expect $600 billion in sales by 2030, which is almost 3X today.

Phil Panaro is the analyst from Boston Consulting Group whose offering that $600 billion bullish sales forecast for NVDA in 2030, based on NVDA maintaining its GPU and AI software (CUDA) lead over time.

He expects that in addition to GPUs, NVDA is going to be selling integrated systems that will be able to generate 40% annual growth in overall AI hardware sales.

Already robotics and cars are generating 70% growth for NVDA and in 2026 Jensen Huang thinks humanoid robots are going to get their ChatGPT moment.

In other words, AI driven humanoid robots will bring AI into the physical world which represents about 95% of the global economy.

  • Intelligence (software based stuff) is 5% of GDP.

In other words, the AI revolution hasn't even begun to get started, we're potentially looking at 20X the addressable market starting next year.

And in the short-terms, despite plenty of risks to what might go wrong with fundamentals, the facts are that 52% EPS growth in 2025 and 25% growth in 2026 is plenty of catalysts for strong returns even if the best-case scenario doesn't occur.

Reason Three: Nvidia Is A Buffett-Style "Fat Pitch"

And here's the rolling average PEs.

Nvidia's median and average PEGY ratio is 0.9. What is it today?

  • Blended 12-Month Growth Rate: 48.30%

  • PEGY Ratio: 0.66

That's a 26% historical discount based on the PEGY ratio.

Justified Return Potential=48.30% growth +34.81% upside to fair value= 83.11% 12-month fundamentally justified return potential.

NOT A FORECAST. This means that "IF AND ONLY IF NVDA grows as expected and returns to historical fair value PEGY ratio, then an 83% increase in the next 12 months is 100% justified by fundamentals".

If NVDA grows EPS by 48% in the next 12 months, returns to fair value, and rises 83%, I can guarantee you most people will be screaming, "BUBBLE!"

I'm telling you now and showing you the math to prove those people wrong and keep you from selling one of the greatest growth stories in history unnecessarily.

Risk Profile: Why Nvidia Isn't Right For Everyone

Let me be very clear that Nvidia's fundamentals, as incredible as they look today, might rapidly deteriorate if worst-case scenarios happen.

For example, the Trump administration has discussed 25% tariffs on chips from non-American companies (like TSMC).

That would directly impact Nvidia and hurt margins, which analysts and investors are concerned about. It might go into effect as early as April 2nd.

  • I consider it a low-probability risk, given that Fabs take 3 to 4 years to build, and US chip makers can't replace TSMC chips anytime soon.
  • TSMC makes 90% of the most advanced chips US companies buy.
  • The US is in an AGI race with China, and this would be unnecessarily shooting ourselves in the foot, and no tariffs (not even 100%) can possibly quicken the pace of fab construction.

However, as seen with the DeepSeek freakout and initial tariff freakouts (one-day flash crashes), Nvidia can plunge far more than the broader market.

This year the S&P's largest peak decline is 3.06%, not even a pullback.

Nvidia? It's still in a 20% bear market!

In the last year, NVDA's peak intra-day decline was -35% (over three weeks culminating in the 16% decline on August 5th, 2024, Japan Yen Carry trade collapse).

Nvidia's bear markets are crashes, with the company nearly being cut in half in its worst declines.

That's not a bug; it's a feature! That's why limit buys on Nvidia are so effective.

When the world loses sight of fundamentals, reacts to headlines, and algos are desperate to sell (at any price), I'm right there to provide liquidity.

  • This is what "being greedy when others are fearful" is all about.
  • We're the buyers of last resort, and why we make so much money when our companies recover.

The biggest fundamental risk to my thesis? That the AI productivity boom turns out to be overblown.

If the actual productivity boost from AI is on the low end of Goldman's analysis, then the "GPU utility" thesis for NVDA could go away.

And if that happens NVDA is still a cyclical hyper-volatile growth company.

Nvidia's EPS growth over the last 20 years has been incredible.

And yet, the median growth rate is negative!

It can see earnings fall as much as 82% in a single year (Great Recession), but even in 2022 (not a recession year), EPS fell 25%, and NVDA fell 65% in that bear market.

In other words, it's easy to "buy the dip" and buy with confidence when the fundamentals are booming, like in 2025 and likely 2026.

But if consensus estimates dive?

NVDA's growth outlook has ranged from 15% to almost 45% in the last few years alone.

And if this chart went back to 1999, You'd likely have seen some pessimistic growth estimates!

In other words, when you own a company, even world-beater blue-chips and Ultra SWANs like Nvidia, you have to remember that there is a lot of faith in management involved.

Most companies that fall 70% or more never recover.

Nvidia has fallen that much twice.

During those times, the fundamental outlook looked much worse than it is today.

Today, Nvidia looks like God's own growth stock. However, I guarantee that will not be the case in the future, even if it does achieve Tom Lee's "10X in 10 years" bull case forecast proves correct.

Bottom Line: NVDA Is 26% Undervalued Vs. Its Historical PEGY Ratio With 52% Consensus Growth And 100% Potential Growth This Year (And 25% to 50% Next Year)

Nvidia isn't going to double its earnings every year, but it might do so this year, and up to 50% next year, if TSMC's scaling plans are successful and NVDA remains supply constrained.

Even if the growth consensus is merely correct (and it doesn't beat expectations like it has by 11% in the last year) that's 52% growth this year and 25% next year, for a company trading at 32X forward earnings.

A 0.66 PEGY ratio right now is far better than the S&P's 1.42 and shows how NVDA is a potentially explosive Buffett-style "fat pitch".

One whose wild volatility is a feature, not a bug, for savvy investors who understand that narratives can shift overnight (DeepSeek Monday crash) but fundamentals usually don't.

I'm not saying "go all in" on NVDA. That would be wildly reckless.

I'm targeting 12.5% max overweight on NVDA and would never recommend anyone go more than 22% into any Ultra SWAN, no matter their confidence or how strong the fundamentals look today.

But whatever your risk profile and optimal allocation for Nvidia, buying before earnings, at a 21% to 26% discount is a very smart move.

I can say that with 80% statistical confidence, that given the best available data we have today, anyone buying NVDA right now will look like a genius in 5 to 10 years.

Just be ready for the wild ride between now and then.
